food_name: Seafood Congee (Fish Fillet and Prawn, Clear Broth Style)
aliases: Seafood Congee (Fish Fillet and Prawn, Clear Broth Style)
region_of_origin: Guangdong (Canton), China; widely popular in Singapore hawker centres and restaurants
food_subgroup: Plain rice congee with sliced fish fillet, prawns, ginger, and sesame oil

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has rice porridge/congee (FOODON:03311069) but does not have a specific entry for seafood-topped congee with fish fillet and prawn. Closest parent: rice porridge with seafood toppings.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice congee with sliced fish fillet and prawn (seafood congee, clear broth style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A003J used as closest parent for rice-based congee/porridge; no exact code exists for seafood-topped congee variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for seafood congee as a specific food concept.

preparation_method: raw seafood added to hot congee and cooked by residual heat
main_ingredients: white fish fillet (sea bass, snapper, or batang/Spanish mackerel)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of seafood congee as served at Singapore hawker centres is approximately 450g, containing a generous portion of rice porridge topped with sliced fish fillet and 2–3 whole prawns.

nutrient_source.title: Seafood Congee (fish fillet and prawn) — Health Promotion Board Singapore, Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for seafood congee (fish fillet and prawn). This is an exact match to the dish as described. HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Rice congee/porridge analogous estimate; University of Sydney GI Database — congee base GI ~86
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports plain rice congee/porridge at GI ~86 (high). Seafood congee GI is estimated at 82, reflecting a modest downward adjustment from the plain congee base GI due to the substantial lean protein contribution from fish fillet and prawns, which slows gastric emptying and attenuates the postprandial glucose response. This is consistent with published evidence that protein co-ingestion reduces the GI of high-GI carbohydrate foods. GL per 450g serving = (82 × 9.2 × 4.5) / 100 = 34.1. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.guidance: Seafood congee is the lowest-sodium common congee variant (155mg/100g vs ~285mg/100g for century egg pork congee); a standard 450g bowl delivers approximately 697mg sodium, which is moderate and acceptable within a 2000mg/day sodium target, but patients with hypertension should avoid adding soy sauce or salt at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~82) and high glycemic load (GL 34.1 per 450g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite the beneficial lean protein from fish and prawns which provides the best GI buffering of all congee toppings, the congee base retains a high GI (~82) and delivers a high glycemic load of 34.1 per standard 450g serving; patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 250g (GL ~19) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to further moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.1g/100g), omega-3 fatty acids from fish, low cholesterol (28mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Seafood congee is a heart-favourable dish with very low saturated fat (0.1g/100g), meaningful EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids from the fish fillet (anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective), low cholesterol at 28mg/100g, and moderate sodium; it is suitable for cardiovascular disease patients when consumed without added salt or soy sauce and within a standard 450g portion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids from fish; selenium 18mcg/100g; vitamin D 1.2mcg/100g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Seafood congee is the preferred congee option for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids from the fish fillet (anti-inflammatory), selenium (18mcg/100g) and vitamin D (1.2mcg/100g) from fish and prawns which support immune regulation, and very low saturated fat (0.1g/100g).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood Congee (海鮮粥, Hoi Sin Juk) is a delicate, clear-style Cantonese rice porridge dish that is a staple of Singapore hawker centres and Cantonese restaurants. It features a plain white rice porridge base — cooked by simmering rice in a large volume of water or light stock until the grains break down into a smooth, silky, slightly viscous porridge — topped with thin slices of fresh white fish fillet (typically sea bass, snapper, or batang/Spanish mackerel) and 2–3 whole prawns. The seafood is added raw to the hot congee just before serving and cooks through in the residual heat, remaining tender and barely cooked, which preserves the natural sweetness and delicate texture of fresh seafood. The dish is garnished simply with julienned ginger, sliced spring onion, a drop of sesame oil, and white pepper. It is the lightest, most delicate, and most nutritionally optimal of all common congee varieties. Nutritionally, it provides 65 kcal per 100g, 5.8g lean protein, 9.2g carbohydrate, and only 0.8g fat per 100g. The fish and prawn protein meaningfully buffers the high-GI congee base (GI ~82), making it the preferred congee option for patients who must consume congee. It is the lowest-sodium common congee variant at 155mg/100g. The fish provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids, selenium (18mcg/100g), and vitamin D (1.2mcg/100g), making it particularly beneficial for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients. Clinically, it is suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients but should be portion-controlled for type 2 diabetes due to its high glycemic load (GL 34.1 per 450g serving). It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from an exact match entry in the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (plain congee GI ~86, adjusted to 82 for seafood protein buffering effect), with confidence 0.9. All mandatory multilingual names are present. All hard gates pass.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for seafood congee is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry; omega-3 content is qualitatively noted from fish fillet but quantitative MUFA/PUFA split is not available in the cited source. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source — no trans fats in this minimally processed seafood dish). GI of 82 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database plain congee GI (~86), adjusted downward to reflect the protein-mediated GI buffering effect of fish fillet and prawn co-ingestion, consistent with published evidence on protein-carbohydrate co-ingestion and postprandial glycaemia. GL per 450g serving calculated as (82 × 9.2g/100g × 450g) / 100 = 34.1.
